In almost everything, I just finished the No BS Marketing Thea Fluent book and the case history we have in the back of the book is from a, this made me think of it, is from a eyewear company in the UK. Their average sale is $5,000 and they have a completely diagnostic consultative sales process. They have probably the only optical store in the world with no eyeglasses visible when you come into the store. So, there are certain things people have to know that if they just sort of rush them to the sales point and surprise them with the idea of a $5,000 tab for eyeglasses, they are not going to close very many sales. I don't care how good the salesperson is. So, the lead up has to take these things into consideration. I was watching TV the other day on a show on Esquire Channel and it was... This is after the view? After the view in Ellen. They call me Johnny and he was in Paris, France and he was at a high-end boutique and part of the high-end boutique had waters and it was a wall of water. You talk about the most commoditized thing on the planet, water is it, but they brought out a person to diagnostically interview him about what is the right water for him to purchase. It was a series of questionnaires. It's almost like a Sama'a'a that is coming in asking about the wine. But this is for water. The wall was as big as the wall behind us and the questions were long and then they prescribed the correct water for him for that moment in time, for that meal. You talk about the most commoditized thing on the planet. Well, I can commoditize it more and it's automated. So it's not that sophisticated. But Dr. Scholes figured out, instead of selling you foot inserts off a rack at Walgreens, if you stand on a machine first and it takes a diagnosis of your feet and it then tells you blue-19 is yours, they can charge twice as much as they could before for the same product. So, you know, diagnostic selling can be applied at a lot of different levels. And if you marry it to getting the deal breakers taken care of through the process, rather than having them be ugly barriers that you're trying to drag somebody over at the very end. And if you are going to use a sales force, so you, I, or he may be good enough to sell that way. I am, I can't speak for you two guys, but I am. I can let it get all the way to a 12-foot high barrier with barbed wire on the top. I can drag this out of a bench over the barrier. I don't like doing it and I don't have to do it hardly at all anymore. But I'm that good. You are not going to get 100 people to work for you who are that good. It's unscalable. So you've got to have a sales process that works for the worst guy you've got, not just the best guy you've got, because you can't get enough best guys. Yeah, absolutely. One of the things that it seems to be, I'm just glancing at some of the questions that are coming up is, my business is a different kind of thing. So again, this goes to marketing. You know, well, I can't do risk reversal in my business because I'm this. I can't send out something in advance of the presentation. I mean, before meeting with somebody. The only reason they can't is if the leap from advertising marketing to selling is so compressed and they're afraid to open it back up. Right. That's. Okay, got you. Got you. But let's address this because again, it's the same thing that's coming up again, which is my business is different. My selling is different. How would you guys address that? Because it's not. There's almost no circumstance that all of this strategic, not just tactical approach can't be applied to. So the problem of the immediate sale has been solved in all sorts of businesses in the death business.
